Strategies: Borrowing money from family or friends
Experience: I live with my parents partly / mainly BECAUSE I can't afford to live on my own. I'm OK, but I've made sacrifices in the structure of my life so that I can keep working the job I love even though I don't get paid enough to support myself. My "earned income" on my taxes the first year I worked as a TPT for UNM was around $13,000. My parents live 1 hour away from UNM so everyday I work, I commute 2.5hrs (including walking time to my car because I have to park far away so I don't pay for parking). With gas prices high, my gas cost per semester ends up costing what I get paid for 1 credit hour. That means 1 credit hour goes directly to gas. The commute also takes precious time out of my work day and as a result I am always exhausted. I often wake up between 2am-4am to work and often don't get to sleep until around 11pm. This impacts the quality of thought I can offer to my students and do my job well. The drive can also be unsafe and when the weather is bad I have to drive in dangerous conditions or not come to work; just this past Monday night (I teach until 8pm) I was driving home and almost got ran off the road twice and almost got in an accident due to swerving on ice on the highway. I can't take the train because I have to work so late at night. When I was a graduate student, I rented an apartment in Albuquerque and had a 5minute walking commute. I did feel unsafe frequently then due to the neighborhood I could afford to live in. Last year, I couch-surfed at least one night of the week and slept on a friend's couch in her ~500sf house in Albuquerque. It was nice not to drive so much but couch-surfing came with its own challenges and was not restful or ultimately a valid solution. It felt like surviving, not thriving. Also, my car has broken down several times, once on the highway. As a result, I've had to stay at a hotel in Albuquerque and even an inexpensive hotel was "expensive" to me. The uncertainty of these factors is stressful to me and affects my quality of life. Today, for example, I'm stressed worried that I won't be able to drive safely to UNM for class and FROM UNM back home. I feel I need to hold class so I worry I risk my safety getting to UNM. Another thing, when I was a graduate student I could get an apartment. However, now I can't sign a year lease because I'm hired on a semester-contract basis. That job insecurity makes it so I don't want to take the risk of signing a year lease and then getting stuck with it and jobless if I'm not hired again the next semester. I also can't get a regular bank loan to buy a house (not that I can afford it anyway) because the bank doesn't view my series of semester contracts as valid employment history. This is a bit unrelated to food and housing insecurity, but is part of the TPT/adjunct inequity and does affect my personal health and well-being: the fact that benefits are linked to the start and end dates of my employment contract means I often go without health insurance. I have to choose to pay for a full month (say August) of health insurance even though the coverage only starts the first day of the semester (around August 20) or start my coverage in September. My health insurance ends in May, leaving me without coverage for June and July unless I work 0.75FTE over the summer (which, by the way, the way the credits work out either gives me just under 0.75FTE or way over because I have to take on another class to bump me over the 0.75FTE - it would be better if the FTE requirement for benefits was better attuned to what credits we are offered). Also, as a TPT I don't have access to use the UNM recreational facilities. I solved this by using my tuition remission benefit to become a student again and as a student I get access to SHAC and the gym, if I need them. Overall, I've had to be very creative to create complex work-arounds in many facets of my life to make it so I can work at UNM and live a decent life. I don't see this as a long-term solution (and there is no career path forward for a TPT anyway) and UNM will ultimately lose me as an employee. I hope through my story it is clear how it is not a simple question of just food or housing insecurity as the metrics of the imposition, inequity, and injustice of how UNM treats TPT employees. Everything is connected and something that might look like one thing is actually the result of something else. Thank you for providing the opportunity to share our experiences. I really hope this gets better. Right now I feel like I'm killing myself. I feel like the sacrifices I'm making for this job will have long-term health implications (overweight, no time to exercise, don't have the luxury to make healthy eating decisions, mental stress, severe sleep depravation, heart racing all the time, depressive thoughts at times, anxiety) and this worries me a great deal.
Trans_pub_notuse: I do not feel safe,There is no public transportation near my home,Public transportation does not go where I need to go,Other
Trans_pub_notuse_why: It adds even more time to my already long commute. It is unreliable (arrives late). It doesn't run during the times I need it. It is cold and uncomfortable. I can't carry the items I need to carry (large art-like supplies). So many reasons. I'd love to use public transit if it worked.
Disability: Prefer not to answer
Income_other: It depends on how many classes I am offered and accept to teach. This semester I'm teaching 5 classes = 13 credits (which translates to about 108% employment so even though I'm a Temporary part-time TPT employee I'm working more than "full time") so I'm WAY overworked but I need the income and health benefits. My first year teaching my adjusted income on my taxes was around $13,000. This summer I plan to teach 12 credits (4 classes). If I teach about 5 classes per semester EVERY semester (spring, summer, and fall) I might hope to make about $50,000 and maintain full benefits year-round.
OE2: The first thing would be giving TPTs multi-year contracts with full, year-round benefits. If that happens, I would feel able to lease an apartment in Albuquerque. I think that SINGLE move by the university would significantly improve many other co-factors. Another obvious change would be to increase pay. However, I want to be clear that just increasing pay does not solve all the other myriad difficulties of working as a TPT and if I had to choose between a small increase in pay or multi-year contracts with full year-round benefits, I'd choose the contracts over the pay.
OE3: In one of the text boxes I explained this but I want to share it here so it is obvious. I'm temporary part-time faculty but I'm working OVER "full-time". I work more than a full-time employee (depending on the semester). This semester I'm 108% employed. Despite this insane amount of work, I make very little, worry about saving for retirement, don't have any job security, don't have reliable benefits, and feel my only option to make this work is to live with my parents and eat their food.

Obstacles: There is a lack of fresh produce or quality meat goods for people of any financial bracket to purchase in Tucumcari. Often people who can afford it make weekly or bi-weekly trips to Clovis NM, or Amarillo TX, etc., to purchase groceries. Paying for a tank of gas on top of groceries in the current economic climate is too much strain for the poorest citizens in Tucumcari or anywhere else in the state for that matter. There are a few restaurants in town where one could get a healthy meal if they can afford it. Otherwise, the options are all fast food or questionable produce/meat at the grocery store in town. The lack of jobs with any sufficient wages is also a big part of the problem in Tucumcari.

The school does a lot to hold events and provide food to the community and students who attend. However, there is no sustained availability of food offered through the college.

Experience: Housing is one of the biggest problems in the town of Tucumcari. Personally, I am staying with another faculty member while trying to find a rental. In the 5-6 months that I've been here, very little has opened up in terms of rentals. Most of what is advertised is horrible housing often in sketchy places. A good percentage of especially new faculty & staff members are living in hotels, motels, or campers. A lot of students are as well, and I believe currently, there are no dorms for the students. Multiple students have stated that there are hard drug problems in the apartment complexes that they are staying in. Interactions with other tenants who are not in their right minds, constant noise and drama, occasional violence or gunshots, police being called, all put a strain on students just trying to do their schoolwork. OE1: While basic needs have not impeded my ability to work, I see it regularly interfering with students' academic pursuits. Our campus is not within walking distance of many neighborhoods, we do not provide any kind of on-campus housing, there is limited public transportation available, and students often struggle with getting enough food and getting nutrient-dense, healthful food that doesn't lead to later health problems such as diabetes and heart disease. These issues are only multiplied when students have children or other dependents to care for, making it very difficult for them to access education as a pathway to a sustainable wage.
OE2: I am really proud of our campus' recent Food Security Initiative in launching the Campus Cabinet food bank for current and prospective students. I would like to see the Campus Cabinet grow to increase capacity with food pick ups and include basic hygiene and household items such as shampoo, soap, deodorant, detergents, etc. I would also like to see our campus address our lack of on-campus housing. Since we are a community college in a small city serving primarily non-traditional students, I don't know that dorms would be feasible, but I would like to see us partner with local housing programs to provide subsidized housing for students or perhaps launch a roommate-finding program to help students reduce housing costs.
OE3: Thank you for doing this survey!

OE1: I am very resentful to my employer for not paying me better. In Florida I made over double what I am paid here. I do not know how we keep our accreditation and I can't wait to quit this job. It will take me a decade to financially and professionally recover from the two years I have spent here. OE2:
Be more open to diversity when hiring and paying employees and giving that extension to students who may become employees. I have applied to internal jobs for which I am qualified (or very slightly under qualified). There is a clear bias (gender, personality, ability to manipulate) in who is hired. 
Diversity when paying, I have more qualifications, experience, and oversee more projects than anyone however the degree is what determines the pay (I cannot afford to pursue a new degree even if it benefits the college). I can get work dones (when my mind is clear) 2-4 times faster, yet get payed per hour at the same rate. My tasks are complete and I get payed for 1 hour when someone else gets paid for 3 just cause it took them longer. 25% of my pay goes to taxes and more if I had insurance through them. Take home pay is 12 dollars and 13 dollars for many full time workers. 
I do not want free housing (I like my house I just need to afford it). We do not free food ( I see too much free food get thrown away. We need reasonable pay for living and buying what we need. When we pay for it, we endear it. (I am told the benefits outweigh the pay) the benefits are restricted and mean nothing if I cannot live in peace. 
As far as students, go if this continues, what hope do they have but to fall into the same net. They might have free food items today but do they know how to cook with what is free? Do they have the appliance, the cooking place, the utilities, the secure home?

Experience: Although I currently am able to handle all of my basic needs in partnership with my husband, in my past I was homeless three times; all of these instances were times of transition while I was single, in college and moving in and out of different types of housing. I handled the issue by making extensive use of the school's library to study, sleeping on friend's couches or floors, borrowing money until I could find employment, and accepting meals from people when they offered to pay. If I'd had a car, I might have slept in it, but I did not own a car at that time. (Incidentally, I am grateful to every single person who helped me out during that time, and most have remained my life-long friends).

OE1: I am currently not experiencing housing security. Food security is only a supply-chain issue for me at present. Consequently, neither is affecting my work at present.
OE2: Although I do not see it as the college administrator's fault, our campus really needs to identify and secure a reliable food vendor to operate our snack bar which offers accessibly priced, decent food. Perhaps partnering with a local fast-food restaurant to operate a small-scale satellite location of their larger franchise in our on-campus snack bar location might work. I realize McDonald's is not the highest quality food, but it has menu options that are nutritious, and reasonably priced. And, of course, it is popular!
OE3: Although I have reached a place wherein I am secure of my housing and food, it was not always that way in my life. I realize I am fortunate in that working hard, and being frugal paid off and that good habits often do not pay off for everyone in a similar way. I have not forgotten how it felt to be homeless, have no money, and be truly insecure of my basic needs being met. I am not sure anyone could truly "get over it."

I would not be able to go to class because I wouldn't have enough money to put gas in my car so I could drive to class or even to the store to buy cheap food like ramen (in fact, I didn't even know about ramen or how cheap it was). Also, when I was living on campus, I still could not afford the microwavable ramen-in-a-cup because those were roughly $2 or $3 each plus tax, and I didn't have any equipment to cook the 5 cent ramen packages (plus the dorm would not allow hotplates or any such equipment required to cook that type of food). Being hungry and having nothing to eat meant I slept a lot to try and avoid that feeling. And sleeping a lot meant I missed a lot of work and classes, which really affected my GPA and I almost flunked out of college.

Experience: When I first moved to NM to take this job, I had been working multiple parttime jobs to make ends meet. We (hubby and I) didn't have enough money to move our belongings across the country (took a year). He stayed behind to keep working until he could find a job in NM. Meanwhile, I camped on the floor of the house we were renting on a sleeping bag and foam mat. I had the pets with me, one file box of starter teaching materials, a suitcase full of clothes, a laptop, and a director's chair and tray table. At that time, our pay was very low as the campus was in the process of refiguring faculty salaries to make them equitable by factoring education level and years of experience on a matrix. But until that adjustment, which didn't kick in until January, our net pay was very low and the rent in Gallup is high. By the end of the month, I had about $20 left over. I often had meals of lentils cooked in Cup-a-Soup to give them flavor.
Trans_pub_notuse_why: We have a reliable vehicle; I don't need public trans. But there really isn't any convenient public transit in Gallup anyway, especially that would go into developments. You have to walk to main routes to access a bus stop, for example. That's over a mile away.
Disability: Living with a chronic medical condition (i.e. diabetes, high blood pressure, heart disease, etc)
OE1: Rent for housing in Gallup is high; home prices are also expensive. I don't know if I would qualify for a mortgage out here even though I've previously owned 2 other homes. But if I did, the money we saved between the rent and the mortgage would go to student loans. I couldn't even use that $ to offset the costs of inflation/COLA.
OE2: Our campus definitely needs a food pantry for students. Our bookstore should offer healthy food that's fresh (not expensive prepackaged sandwiches or plastic cups of Chips Ahoy or bags of junk food). It definitely needs to work with community/civic leadership to help bring down the costs of housing, either with rent controls, new home building, underwritten housing for interested faculty, or something like that. One idea that's floating is a campus garden - that produce could be given away or sold at a discount. A stronger collaboration with the local community pantry/garden to allow students a chance to grow some produce could help. Also, what about using AmeriCorps or FoodCorps volunteers to help build programs, lead these efforts, etc.?

Obstacles: Only our cooperative grocery store sells primarily organic, healthy food. Its hours and size are not as convenient as other grocery stores who sell 10% organic offerings. Also, the food served to my child in public school is pre-packaged, sugar added (especially chocolate milk and he says no white milk is available most days!), oily, low quality, cheap, trucked-in, rather than fresh, food, so I send a home lunch with him at least two days per week. He wants to fit in, so I allow him to eat this very poor quality food we serve our American children 2-3 times per week. Most days he says it is gross or unappetizing. He came from a private school who had their own garden and made fresh food for the children, in their kitchen, every day. My public high school growing up made much of their food on site. Why has that changed? Why are we skimping and buying only Sysco pre-packaged, canned food for our children now? What has happened to the budget for food in the public school system?
Foodpantry_reasons: I do not need assistance with obtaining food and household supplies
Foodavail: The cafeteria food we serve our students is shockingly poor quality, very similar to the food the public schools serve my son and other K-12 students. Pre-packaged (even hard boiled eggs, for Pete's sake, on which you can taste the chemical preservative because the eggshell is already peeled), trucked-in, plastic bags cut open and heated. There is almost no fresh preparation of items, so student choose the frozen-flash fried options because they taste better, such as the fries and hamburgers, which are available every day. Not even the hamburger patties are made on sight, I guess because it is cheaper to buy them pre-made than to pay a student worker a living wage to make fresh food? They make one hot item that "meets" the nutritional standards each day, but it is put together from bagged and canned ingredients. Only the lettuce is reasonably fresh most days. We have only one other food option on campus: a bagel shop. You can get some healthy, fresh bagel sandwiches there, as well as fruit smoothies, and I am grateful for that, but it is pretty expensive for students.

Foodavail: Plenty of healthy food options at Taos Cafe. As a new staff member I haven't yet had the time or opportunity to visit the other food locations on campus. The university needs to receive funding specifically dedicated to supporting student basic needs to support basic needs staff and services for students. The pantry on campus should be open more than 2 days for 3 hrs each day to be most accessible to the greatest number of students in need. Also, the university needs more funding for paying for the food accessed from the local food pantries, or the state's regional food pantries need more fiscal support from state government in order to provide their food free of charge to community partners. Additionally, students connecting with food resources on campus should have dedicated Basic Needs Case Managers on campus that they can reach out to for basic needs/economic crisis support beyond food resources (i.e. housing support, SNAP application assistance, etc.)

OE2: Permanently fund one or two full time Student Affairs Basic Needs Case Manager positions to provide comprehensive support to students experiencing chronic or acute basic needs insecurity. Fund one or more part time positions, or one full time position, solely supporting the campus pantry/food distribution program on campus. Implement and promote a charitable giving campaign with the funds committed solely to supporting the pantry and emergency assistance funding for students (perhaps one account for each). Promote/establish opportunities for payroll deduction for employees to give to these accounts.
OE3: The prevalence of poverty and food insecurity in the state and DoÃ±a Ana County dramatically highlight the need for basic needs support for our students in higher education in the state. OE2: Housing is an enormous issue in Santa Fe.  Faculty cannot afford to live in the area that they serve, thus commuting often long distances.
OE3: The pay is insufficient even for faculty who have been here many years.  Students, who we encourage to get an education, see people with 10 + years of university and PhDs earning less than a manager at a Starbucks with a high school diploma.  They take note of this and basically think we are stupid to go into a profession that pays so little.  The college's view is that if anyone in teaching wants to earn good money, get out of teaching as fast as possible, and go into administration because that's where the money is.  But, what if teaching is our gift, and we want to share that, but also earn a decent salary--the college doesn't care, the Board and the Legislature doesn't have a clue, and they think administrators are more qualified (nothing could be further from the truth) or that they have more responsibility . . . but they are constantly saying that we, the faculty, are face-to-face with students and that's the most important thing, but obviously it isn't because they won't pay us what we're worth.  So many people are leaving this profession because of poor treatment.  NEVER say "people don't go into education for the money, it's a vocation" NO IT ISN'T, it's a job, a job that requires years of graduate school.  We earn and deserve decent pay--we're not Mother Theresa, we're people who want to look after our families.

Food / housing programs are great, but we also need to make work pay.

Before, I would pick up extra shifts because I needed money to pay rent, get gas, or buy groceries and I would be struggling to work so much and complete assignments. I would always get the full amount on my student loans so that I could use that to pay down my credit card a little more because the payments are so crippling sometimes and keep the extra for more basic necessities. OE1: I know that I do not have a lot of extra money to spend on food, so I try to buy just what I need and have foods that go a long way and do not cost as much. This does not effect my work very much, but I do have an extra job in addition to my full time job which I use for groceries throughout the week to lessen my stress over affording food. I have less free time and sometimes go to bed much later than I would want because I get stuck at my other job.
OE2: Healthier options would be amazing. Having it also be affordable, because healthier food options always seem to be much more expensive. If UNM worked with the state to see if more of their students could easily qualify for food stamps because school takes time away from work (and classes are often during peak work days and hours). Or even make the SNAP benefits at the state level easier for all college students. I tried for a couple of years to get food stamps and never qualified, yet affording food for myself was so stressful. Being single makes it much harder to qualify than it would be for a single parent or family, yet that population sometimes needs help, too. In our country a college education is such a privilege because there are so many obstacles.
OE3: I have a good support system and am lucky enough to receive Medicaid, but if I did not have those I cannot imagine where I would be. Many people attending universities have a lot less. UNM has some programs to help their students, but they are still very expensive which is by far the biggest obstacle for students. I attended college there in my mid 20s so I had no scholarship even though I was a great student with great grades. I now have student loans I will be paying off for the next few decades. Making college classes less expensive, having cheaper parking passes, and better scholarships would go a long way to making housing and food more secure. SO much money went into my education and even more with interest will, too. Books, supplies, transportation, less working hours, and time away from home all cost money, too.

Experience:
I recently found out that my rent is going up by over $150 a month unless I move. I was distraught because I love the place I live right now, and I have no savings for a deposit on a new place. I borrowed over $500 from a close friend (who has the money because he lost his place to live, and his parents aren't charging him rent), and about $200 from my parents so I could pay the deposit, fees, and first month's rent on a tiny apartment in an unsafe part of town. 
This move, along with working full time and taking 9 credit hours a semester is stressing me out. I worry that rent will just keep going up and that I will have to find a new job that pays more and drop out of school just to afford my necessities.  In addition, I'm having to sell some of my furniture and valuables, which is eating into my time to focus on class. I often skip my evening class because I have to pack. I took two full days off of work to move (thank goodness UNM is so generous will annual leave). 
If I'm, as lucky and privileged as I am, experiencing these things, how is someone much less fortunate than myself getting by? How are people who can't borrow from friends and family doing this?
Disability: Living with a learning disability,Living with a mental health condition,Other
OE1: It makes me wonder what the purpose of working is if I still can't afford my necessities even though I work full time. I want to call in- a lot.
OE2: I encourage the university to consider paying their staff more. $16.85 isn't enough anymore. That isn't generous like it once was, and I'm afraid that I'll have to move back in with my parents at 23 because the cost of living is going up faster than my salary is.

Obstacles: Recently food prices have gone up.  I noticed that my grocery bill has been a little higher even though I am buying the same kinds of foods.
Foodpantry_reasons: Other students need this help more than I do
Foodavail: I put 0 to 5 dollars as the cost of a nutritious meal on campus, but the reality is that there is no such thing as a nutritious meal option on our campus.  We currently do not have a cafeteria on campus, so the only food options are snacks from vending machines and the bookstore.  These snacks just small snacks and not meals.  They are also highly processed foods, so if anyone wants to eat something nutritious, they must either bring it from home or eat off campus.  When we did have a small cafeteria on campus, there were not as many cheaper options on the menu.  Prices were not too high, but there was not a variety of differently priced options for whole meals that were not snacks or sides.  There were also not very many nutritious options or options for people with dietary restrictions.  They were also not consistent with their hours, so sometimes people would come in that morning, but would not be able to have breakfast because it was closed.  This happened to me once before as well.  That being said, that food vendor is no longer on campus.  However, we have just started up a food pantry, so students will at least have some more options of obtaining food.  This is a good thing and I am happy that this change was implemented and it bodes well for our future.  We started a community garden as well, so as that grows there should not only be more food availability on campus, but also more nutritious options.  Overall, I think we are improving a little in the area of food availability.

Strategies: Seeking out events with free food,Something else not listed (please share)
Strategies_other: Received food from family to lighten the grocery bill for that week in order to have a meal or two that I wouldn't have to plan for at the grocery store.
Experience: I have not had an experience like this.
Trans_pub_notuse_why: I usually do not need public transportation.
Disability: No disability or chronic medical condition
Race: Black or African American,White or Caucasian
OE1: Currently, my work is not affected by food or housing insecurity on a personal level.  Since I work on a college campus, I affected by others having food or housing insecurity by trying to understand ways concerning how to best help them.
OE2: Right now we are doing a good job starting up the food pantry for students, and working on a community garden.  I know that some of my co-workers either work or have had to work multiple jobs in order to live sustainably, so having more conversations about that and coming up with solutions would be good.  While having good food security at home is the best option, I think having a cafeteria with consistent hours and reasonable food prices could potentially be helpful for our student and staff population.  As for housing, we don't offer anything that I am aware of to address that issue.  We can only point others to outside resources that are available.
OE3: I cannot think of anything else to share.

OE2: I'm not sure what the school could do that it isn't already doing.  We have a pantry.  We discuss the issue and try to be flexible with students.  The issue I see is housing prices on par with much larger cities with higher pay.  So few people make enough to pay rent in the thousands here.  Housing is not a commodity dictated by supply and demand.  We are stuck paying what is charged in order to have something, cramming more and more people together, and those who cant end up homeless.  Then we wonder why so many people are homeless.  The city and state are more capable to implement solutions than the university and ought to be doing more.

OE1: I have relocated in order to find a job that pays enough for the cost of living in my area.
OE2:
Give raises, hire staff that have commensurate skills for the job, pay people what their skills are worth in the competitive market.  

The State Auditor should do a salary evaluation for all State Universities and make the salaries and wages consistent.  NMT employees are paid 20%-25% lower than their counterparts at UNM and NMSU.

Housing in Socorro is sub par in many cases and controlled by a small group of people who conspire to set rent prices for their tenants according to their salaries which are made public.  The University should consider that rental housing is both expensive and limited when determining employment offers; or, alternatively, work with local realtors to assist in setting fair prices for students and employees.

Public utilities in Socorro are extremely expensive with no visibility as to how rates are determined.  As NMT is the largest customer in the city, it has the ability to question the rates and ample staff to pursue answers to questions on rates. 

Many NMT employees attain high levels of management with high salary levels due to seniority or nepotism and do not have the requisite skills or education for their positions.  This is a high risk for the university and prevents qualified people from accessing these positions in the long term.

OE3: I am fortunate to be in a well-paid position and a two-income household. There are still many elements of financial security that are difficult... elderly parents that need financial assistance, expenses of getting college-age kids through school, high inflation, etc. The "American Dream" that is presented as a goal in media and public discourse seems to require multiple times the salary that I earn, and I think I'm a relatively high wage earner in NM. In spite of my salary, I'm still living paycheck to paycheck, and am unable to put meaningful contributions into savings. For any struggles that I have, I imagine it must be much harder for those who earn less, and it is really important for the country as a whole to address the growing divide between the 1% and everyone else.

OE2:
There are many homeless on the campus.  A community shelter would make the campus more secure.  For these individiuals.

Cost of housing and meal plans are too high, a supplemental program like sliding scale or food stamp applicability to dining halls might get better food to students.

OE3:
I Having been a student in the bad old days some of the issues with food was that I did not qualify for any assistance,  My undergraduate was from Texas A&M and they had a great program from former students that supplied loans that did not have interest to pay tuition which could be paid off at the end of semester.  There were enough jobs around that I could work several and pay it off.  Graduated without debt.  Here there are not enough jobs even though the city is much bigger and federal loans is the only way, especially for graduate students, to make ends meet.  The grad students at Texas had health insurance which eased my burden.  Of course, the state had approved such a thing.  

One of my biggest peeves was from last years ice storm.  Students were stranded on campus and UNM did not do anything.  Calling school closed late and no preparations was very dangerous.  I was busing students to my daughters trailer since she lives on south campus.  CIty buses were stranded and I only found out later that the sun van has 4-wheel drive.  Still there were students that were stuck at the library and had no way home and the library was closing.  I would love to see a plan if students are trapped where they can shelter until the danger passes and they can get off campus.

I would support slowing down growth in other areas in order to combat food insecurity, but I don't know what such support would look like. A university-run restaurant with subsidized food costs would put pressure on private, in-sourced vendors, reducing the options for those who don't need the help.

OE2: Food and Housing insecurity is a wide spread issue and has a certain class stigma attached to it. The best way to overcome stigmas is to talk about the issue, not about who or why. I believe our places of public gatherings should offer space for the community to provide for themselves. These places that were built for the community should have community gardens and instead of decorative vegetation, food producing trees and plants should be in abundance. In the harvest season, the community should be able to use a shared space to practice food preservation. In the off season (winter) there should be green houses or high tunnels where winter crops could be grown. Extra food should be given to the food pantries. It is crazy to me that 50% of the food is thrown in the trash while others do not know where their next bite will come from.

Experience:
I do not know if my experience matches housing insecurity, because I am sheltered with my parents. But, I am preparing to move out. I have a stable, secure job and 43k yearly salary before taxes, but the number of places that match what I could put to rent is almost non-existent in Santa Fe and small in Albuquerque and surrounding areas. I make too much to apply to income-restricted apartments, but too little to live in much beyond a studio. I do not want to partner up with a roommate, either. I SHOULD be able to live alone, and have a bedroom. These conditions pushed me to look out of the state. 

This is my motivation for finishing an M.S., to get more, but in looking for places to live and finding none, I have certainly been given over to anxiety and bouts of sadness, and of course it's taken time from being able to read for class or want to sit down and do homework rather than play games or watch something that helps me escape. It's caused fights, which also distract from work. I don't want to be stuck here--but how is it that I can have a respectable job that pays decently, if not competitively, and not be able to stand on my own two feet?
Disability: Living with a mental health condition
Gender2: Other or prefer not to say
OE1: It is a distraction and it depresses motivation. Thinking about what I am going to have to do once I am done with school lingers on my mind a lot, which sucks focus. But, also, the feeling that I might have to leave to be able to make enough to have a space makes me question why I work as hard as I do. It also has the effect of impacting my loyalty, not just my work ethic, but how positively I regard my workplace.
OE2: The simplest implementation, though a large demand, would be to increase wages.
OE3: I see opportunity in our numerous empty buildings to create shelter, that is managed. We have the space, we just need the will.

It would only feed the employee/student lunch and not their families, but it would be based on income and family size, etc. Not sure how to implement. We have catered events on campus. OE1: I am very concerned about students and trainees impacted by housing and food insecurity, particular medical students and residents. There are fewer supports in place for professional students than undergraduates, their schedules are extremely intense, they are likely to be in high debt situations, their work hours are extremely long so many services would be closed when the students would be able to access them, and there is great stigma about reaching out for help.
OE2: Graduate and professional students should be intentional targets for support and awareness campaigns. A food pantry site on north campus with long hours (or an open fridge/pantry that could be accessed at any time), grant money to help trainees with temporary housing, dedicated social workers and basic needs supporters for north campus to help manage the case loads. I believe once the needs are really investigated, graduate and professional students will be a very high needs population.
OE3: The intersection of housing/food insecurity and intimate partner violence is very strong. People who are leaving abusive relationships may have high, accute, time sensitive needs, and we need interim as well as long term supports for them, and the current supports through the Women's Resource Center are wonderful but extremely understaffed and underfunded. They cannot support all of the need that exists.

OE1: Right now I am not impacted. I can pay our mortgage, but I worry about losing my job in a recession.  I also realize funds are limited for home repairs, etc. Also, I am concerned about the issues that particularly SFCC students and adjunct faculty are facing. It's obvious the reason that some of our students have left the college is because they had to leave Santa Fe and cannot afford to live here. They often move to Albuquerque or further away. Sometimes they can handle the commute, but it interferes with their ability to even have a part-time job. As gas prices rise, they will not want to commute great distances to finish their degrees.
OE2: Offer regular food service with discounted (affordable)and/or free meals. Loss of cental food services have left many feeling isolated and students leave campus as soon a class is over, since there is no longer a place to visit with friends and eat on a regular basis.  State would need to address these solutions: full coverage of tuition, fees and books to free up funding for living expenses. Housing needs to be addressed by the city and state. While dorms are not usually considered for community colleges, I think if SFCC could offer limited dormitories for students who are required to spend large amounts of time in person on campus such as nursing students. I also think it would be worth exploring if the nearby IAIA campus has any empty dorm rooms that could be made available to SFCC students, but we'd need to find funds to help students with those expenses. It would also be helpful if we had more emergency funds available to students who need to move, etc. We do offer some limited funds via the SFCC Foundation, but I don't think they are authorized to give the amounts that would be needed to pay first and last month's deposit before someone is evicted. Also, if the state could assist with offering covered or more affordable health insurance for employees it would free up money for food and housing expenses. In general, rental options are very expensive and limited. Santa Fe seniors are on the waiting list for housing assistance for years. Young people do not have access to any inexpensive housing options. Santa Fe is slowly becoming a community where the poor and middle class have no where to live.
OE3: I hope the state legislature will prioritize higher education as a way for students to gain access to better paying jobs as well as become more rounded and informed citizens. I'm glad elementary education and high school education are being more supported. But individuals working in higher education in New Mexico do not feel valued. I am concerned since I've seen staff and faculty leave the college to go work in the elementary and high schools since they can earn more pay there. They have more steps to earn higher wages, etc. in the lower-level schools. Our college needs to retain quality staff, adjunct and full-time faculty to meet the challenges in our community.

OE1: If food or housing is not safe, it can affect an employee's work in many ways. For example, people who live in unsafe housing may struggle to get enough rest or suffer from health conditions due to inadequate living standards. Low-quality food can also lead to malnutrition or illness, which can disrupt one's work performance. Additionally, if a family is financially strained due to high housing costs or the need to purchase safe housing, the employee may struggle to focus at work or use too much of their salary just to meet basic needs.
OE2: Colleges and universities can help address food and housing insecurity in several ways. These can include providing emergency food and housing assistance, increasing access to affordable housing, providing free food pantries, and offering education and programs to help students better manage their finances. Additionally, schools can partner with outside organizations to provide students with additional resources, such as legal aid and mental health support.

Experience: The stress of worrying about how I am going to buy food and pay the bills impacts my work performance.  There is an ongoing fear about how I am going to get through this, how am I going to make ends meet?  I often am in meetings talking about these issues in our community and it is difficult because it is an emotional topic for me and I don't know if I should talk about my struggles or not. In one meeting, where I did comment on my financial struggles, a person in a leadership role told me that I need to find a husband. Everyone laughed, but it wasn't funny for me. I turned in my parking pass b/c I cannot afford to pay for it anymore.  Also relevant: I need medical care on an ongoing basis, and I can't afford my treatments even though I have this job with health insurance.   Spending more money on healthcare = less money for food and bills. This impacts my work b/c I have higher pain levels when I cannot get treatment and end up using more sick leave as a result.
Disability: Living with a chronic medical condition (i.e. diabetes, high blood pressure, heart disease, etc),Living with a mental health condition
Race2: Other or prefer not to say
OE1: I am not able to put forth my best self and my best work when I am hungry and stressing about how to pay the bills.  We are judged on how we "show up" and it's hard when your clothes are old, you need a haircut, you can't contribute to an office gift or potluck, can't afford the healthcare that you need, etc. I know that I could be doing more in my work if I wasn't carrying the weight of this stress with me all the time and I had the money that I need to take care of myself.
OE2:
Better pay - equitable employment practices. Pay people for the work they are doing. Example: My responsibilities are manager level but my title and pay are not. 

Let people decide how much money to contribute to retirement.
OE3: Thank you for asking these questions. Many of us are stuck btw. not qualifying for assistance and not being able to pay the bills.  We need more services on a sliding scale. I felt like I had to tiptoe around the bedroom and bathroom area which had a shared wall with him.  The other next-door neighbor has an alcohol and deafness problem and is diabetic. Experience: This experience has not been in the past 12 months, but when I was first divorced, I had to start all over and mortgage a new home for myself and my children.  I lost my job and almost lost my home.  I found part-time work until I finally found a full-time job almost a year later.  During that year that I was jobless, I quit paying all my bills except the electricity, gas and groceries because that was all I could afford and that is what we absolutely needed to survive. My house was about to be forclosed on, but thankfully for the pandemic (ironically), the banks had many programs to help homeowners.  Thankfully, today I am blessed to say that I have a great job and I am able to pay all of my bills.  That year really humbled me and nearly broke my spirit.  I learned a lot, especially to never take anything for granted and to never lose your faith.

Experience: I was living with my family in a small town and could not find steady work. I had been paying for college out of pocket, but without a job, it became difficult to cover basic necessities like rent and food. I remember some of my nights going to the library late into the night so I could study and complete my assignments instead of using the little money I sometimes had to buy food. I also often had to choose between paying for certain classes or buying food. It was a very difficult time, and I could feel the stress of knowing that I needed to feed my family but also wanting to stay in school. I kept pushing through, and eventually found work that allowed me to pay for college, but that experience taught me to always be mindful of the struggles faced by those less fortunate.
Disability: No disability or chronic medical condition

I was also hampered by the sense of shame and embarrassment I felt due to my situation, which made it difficult to reach out for help or connect with any of the on-campus resources available to me. Experience: When I was an undergraduate student, I experienced food and housing insecurity that had a direct impact on my ability to go to class or work, focus, study, or complete assignments. I found myself facing a difficult choice between paying for rent and tuition or buying food for myself. I eventually decided to forego paying for rent and instead used the money to purchase enough food to get me through the month. Although this meant that I would have to leave my apartment and stay with friends, I felt that it was necessary to ensure that I had enough to eat. This decision ended up having a major impact on my productivity and overall wellbeing. Without access to my own kitchen, I was unable to cook meals for myself and instead had to rely on takeout or pre-packaged foods. This ultimately made it difficult to concentrate in class or stay motivated to finish my assignments. Additionally, the lack of a consistent living space caused me to feel constantly anxious and overwhelmed. In the end, I was able to manage the hardship and eventually regain stability, but the experience challenged me both academically and emotionally in ways that I had never expected.
Disability: No disability or chronic medical condition
OE1: Community Partnerships: Difficulty in forging partnerships with local organizations that can provide the necessary resources and support needed to address food/housing insecurity.
OE2: Create Financial Education Workshops: Provide workshops that focus on budgeting, debt management, and other topics related to personal finance.

Experience: Because my housing situation is unusual, I will describe it. I currently live in a small travel trailer (about 17 ft long) with my husband on a piece of land we purchased about 50 miles south of Albuquerque, where I work. We chose to do this because renting in the city is very expensive and also unstable - our last landlord tried to evict us for reasons he made up, thinking we would be intimidated and leave. We used the free legal resources for renters available in NM and fought back in order to stay for the rest of the rental agreement. That experience was so stressful that we decided never to rent again. We didn't have enough money for a mortgage on a house, so we bought a piece of land with a well and an electric pole and decided to live in our RV, which is where we are now. I commute 2 hours every day to Albuquerque and back, which is a lot, but at least I am employed. I can afford to buy groceries and gas, and I have health insurance. While I was unemployed, we were technically homeless - living in our RV and moving around from one NM state park to another every 2 weeks, which is much cheaper than renting a space in an RV park, if you 'dry camp'. That means no hookups - no electricity and no running water. So we put out a solar panel and hauled water. I am sharing all this because many people are not aware of this kind of housing insecurity. There are a lot of people who live this way, especially in NM. Also, I just want to say that the money I make as full-time staff is not enough to afford housing of any kind in Albuquerque, unless I spent more than half my paycheck, which is unrealistic. I found a creative housing solution that works for me, but so many people can't, even with full-time jobs.
Trans_pub_notuse_why: I would use the RailRunner if UNM provided more options to and from campus from the downtown Abq stop.
Disability: Living with a mental health condition
OE1: Housing insecurity is no longer an issue for me, but the compromise I reached of living far from the city takes a toll in terms of my time (and gas money).
OE2: Rental subsidies or stipends for students, staff, faculty
OE3: Many full-time UNM staff do not earn a true living wage for Albuquerque in 2023. The pay structure needs to take the crazy inflation of the past few years into account.

Experience: I felt fortunate to have a job, but felt expendable, as if I'd lose that too if my living situation meant I couldn't make it in to work or couldn't perform my tasks well enough. I have health related ADA accommodations, but they never felt enforced, I always felt threatened with my job if I was too sick to make it in. It's gotten somewhat better since I found a safe place to live again, but my chronic illness never give me a day of "okay," and I still feel like I could lose everything if I get too sick, which I often can't control. Medical debt doesn't help, though I wouldn't be able to access any medical care or treatments without the insurance my employer provides, so I'm both grateful and resentful for it. Resentful because I can't live my life without that insurance, and if I lose my job, there go my medications and treatments.

OE3:
I teach one class at a charter high school in the south valley. I take food there regularly because they don't "like" the food the school serves and always complain they are hungry, so they gladly take my packaged food which is primarily junk food. But I figure a treat is a treat, and if they know someone cares, that could mean a lot to them. The systems aren't working for everyone. I'm so lucky to be in such a place of privilege that I didn't earn. I lead a very privileged life compared to the students at the charter school. For example:
There was a shooting a few weeks ago. Two students were killed. Another student misses her dad who left her a decade ago to go to Mexico once he was "wanted" and has come back to the area but hasn't responded that he wants to see her. Another student lost a 2-year-old cousin about 2 weeks ago. Most all live in single parent homes. Apartments. South Valley houses with extended families sometimes. These kids have a very hard life, right here in Albuquerque where we ought to be able to do better. 
I share this because I can see the disparity and it breaks my heart. 
Thanks for initiating this survey to see what needs are out there.

Institution: New Mexico State University, Las Cruces
Obstacles: Adjunct Professor Pay is inhumanly low. I don't know how they calculate their perception of our hours, but it's criminally off because I get paid well below minimum wage. Additionally we are hired by contract only twice a month twice a year. This means I have to save a portion of this insanely low income to survive for three months. Additionally there are not always enough sections for all of the adjuncts to teach every semester, and there are times when I don't even have employment during the entirety of one of those four month periods. I have to live in fear every four months. Additionally, I receive no benefits or perks of any kind and actually have to pay the university for services like parking. This means I am saving nothing for my future. I have worked for NMSU for 12 years and haven't seen a penny raise, as the cost of everything goes up significantly. I also had to take out significant school loans to earn the degree that enables me to have this position, and I am still making payments on those loans.
Foodpantry_reasons: I am not sure I am eligible to use the campus food pantry,Other
Foodpantry_reasons_other: I rely on NM State EBT for my food.
Foodavail: I'm not familiar with the food availability too much because I never eat on campus. I probably couldn't afford it, and I am selective about my diet. I use NM State EBT to purchase food at grocery stores. That is how the EBT money goes the furthest distance.
Unsafe_why: My apartment is exposed to a large open lot. People can see into my home. It is a small place with big sliding windows, and one poor door lock. It is easy to break into. I am an LGBTQIA individual, specifically bi-gender, and my clothing often loudly projects that at home. I have also started to be somewhat public about my identity and my home is quite easy to find. There are others like me living in our complex as well. There has been a significant spike in violence and threats against us both across the nation and here in New Mexico. There are also young people flying images, around this complex, that are associated with homophobia, transphobia, police brutality, and racism. I fear that someone will attack me at any given point. I have heard gunfire in this area on multiple occasions in the last year.
Placesslept: In a rented or owned house, mobile home, or apartment (alone or with roommates/friends)
Strategies: Borrowing money from family or friends,Taking food that has been discarded or left behind
Experience: Where do I start, there are so many. Food is a primary priority, so that is the first place I put my funds, so much of my strife doesn't have to do with food directly, but it has to do with not having enough money to pay for anything else, after food is covered. This has forced me to take up any kind of odd work I can find, like hosting a couple trivia games a month for $50 dollars each, supervising family visitations, playing guitar everywhere I can find for tips, doing two-man outdoor remodeling work in the summer, hosting/teaching meditation classes four times a month for $20 each, assembling furniture, anything I can find. This has puts a high demand on my time, my vehicle, my body, and I have almost nothing left in the way of time to do this high time demand job. I have to grade only 3 assignments for 3 classes of 25 students each per week. If I give each student only 3 minutes of feedback without stopping to stretch or eat, which is impossibly unproductive, that means I spend 10.5 hours on grading alone. That does not include Canvas page update, lesson plans, in class teaching, recording and developing web material for the class, reading the textbook, student meetings, or other departmental demands, It got to the point that I had to cancel two classes and be questioned for future employment by my supervisor who told me "In a just society, adjuncts wouldn't have to work in the conditions they do."
Trans_pub_notuse_why: My schedule is so random I need to be able to come and go at a moment's notice. I drive a highly problematic Dodge Dakota 2003, almost 200,000 miles on it, and do almost all repairs on it myself. It still often presents problems that leave me on foot.
Disability: Living with a mental health condition
OE1: Where do I begin? Stress, anxiety, depression, lack of time, lack of energy, lack of hope, lack of mental health, lack of support or understanding from work.
OE2: Pay adjunct professors and graduate students a livable income.
